content.problemStatement:
There is a rectangle with the left top cell as (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) and the right bottom cell as (Nx,Ny)(N_x, N_y)(Nx​,Ny​). There are MMM circles such that their centers are inside the rectangle where the center of ithi^{th}ith circle will be (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) and the radius of each circle will be RRR.

Find if it is possible that we can move from (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) to (Nx,Ny)(N_x, N_y)(Nx​,Ny​) without touching any circle.

Note:


We can move to any adjacent cell which shares a side or corner with its current cell. That means if you are at the cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) of the grid, you can go to either of the cells (i+1,j)(i + 1, j)(i+1,j), (i−1,j)(i - 1, j)(i−1,j), (i,j+1)(i, j + 1)(i,j+1), (i,j−1)(i, j - 1)(i,j−1), (i+1,j+1)(i + 1, j + 1)(i+1,j+1), (i+1,j−1)(i + 1, j - 1)(i+1,j−1), (i−1,j+1)(i - 1, j + 1)(i−1,j+1), (i−1,j−1)(i - 1, j - 1)(i−1,j−1) if that inside the rectangle.
A circle doesn't touch a cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) if the Euclidian distance from its center to (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is greater than RRR.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NxN_xNx​, NyN_yNy​.
The second line contains two space-separated integers MMM, RRR — the number of circles, radius of each circle.
The third line contains MMM space separated integers X1,X2,…XMX_1, X_2, \ldots X_MX1​,X2​,…XM​ — the XXX-coordinate of circles.
The fourth line contains MMM space separated integers Y1,Y2,…YMY_1, Y_2, \ldots Y_MY1​,Y2​,…YM​ — the YYY-coordinate of circles.




Output Format
For each test case, output 111 if it is possible that we can move from (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) to (Nx,Ny)(N_x, N_y)(Nx​,Ny​) without touching any circle otherwise output 000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We can reach (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) using path (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(0,3)→(1,3)→(2,3)(0, 0) \rightarrow (0, 1) \rightarrow (0, 2) \rightarrow (0, 3) \rightarrow (1, 3) \rightarrow (2, 3)(0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(0,3)→(1,3)→(2,3).
Test case 222: There is no path present.

solution.approach:
In this approach, we directly perform a **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** on the grid. At each step, before adding a neighboring cell to our queue, we check if the cell is safe by iterating over all of the circles and verifying the condition:

(x−Xi)2+(y−Yi)2>R2.(x - X_i)^2 + (y - Y_i)^2 > R^2.(x−Xi​)2+(y−Yi​)2>R2.**Steps Explained:**


**Starting Cell Check:** First, we check if the starting cell (0,0)(0,0)(0,0) is safe. If it is touched by a circle, we cannot even begin, so we return 0.
**BFS Traversal:** From the current cell, we try moving to all 8 possible adjacent cells. For each neighbor:

Ensure it lies within the rectangle bounds.
Check that the cell is not visited.
Confirm that it does not lie within the danger zone of any circle.


**Termination:** If we reach (Nx,Ny)(N_x,N_y)(Nx​,Ny​), we immediately return 1; otherwise, if all possible cells are exhausted, return 0.
This approach has an advantage of being simple to implement without additional memory overhead for precomputation because safety is checked on the fly.

Given that the grid size is relatively small (maximum of about 101×101101 \times 101101×101 cells), we can precompute a **safe grid**. In this grid, each cell is marked as safe or unsafe by checking the condition

(x−Xi)2+(y−Yi)2≤R2(x - X_i)^2 + (y - Y_i)^2 \le R^2(x−Xi​)2+(y−Yi​)2≤R2for every circle.

**Steps Explained:**


**Precompute the Safety Grid:**

For every cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) in the rectangle, iterate over all circles.
If any circle satisfies the unsafe condition, mark the cell as unsafe.


**BFS Traversal:**

Once the grid is precomputed, you only need to check the safety status of a cell (a constant time lookup) while performing a standard BFS.
Start from (0,0)(0,0)(0,0)—if it’s unsafe, return 0 immediately.
Try moving in all 8 directions, and if a neighbor is safe and within bounds, proceed with the BFS.


**Termination:**

If (Nx,Ny)(N_x,N_y)(Nx​,Ny​) is reached, return 1; otherwise, return 0 if no path exists.


This approach separates the costly safety-check from the traversal and may be conceptually easier to understand since the grid’s status is precomputed.

Code ImplementationBelow are the complete implementations for both approaches in **C++** and **Python**. The C++ solutions strictly follow the provided template.
